title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for 3D Object Tracking

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D object tracking in sparse point clouds, featuring two key contributions: a shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV localization approach. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets. While the technical approach is sound and well-motivated, some claims regarding superiority in sparse point cloud handling lack full experimental justification, and ablation studies require expansion.

**Strengths:**  
1.  The work addresses the important and challenging problem of 3D object tracking in sparse point clouds.
2.  The paper is clearly written and well-organized, making the methodology easy to follow.
3.  Sufficient technical detail is provided to understand the proposed architecture and its components.
4.  The approach is technically sound, with clear motivation provided for the design choices, including the shape-aware feature learning and voxel-to-BEV localization.
5.  The experimental results are impressive, demonstrating a significant performance improvement over previous state-of-the-art methods by a large margin on benchmark datasets.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:**  
While the core contributions are valuable and the results strong, several aspects require further validation to fully support the claims made:  
1.  **Justification for Sparse Point Cloud Handling:** The claim of superior performance on sparse point clouds is not fully substantiated by the presented results. Specifically, Table 1 vs. Table 2 comparisons indicate that while the proposed method shows a performance drop on sparse objects (e.g., Cyclist/Pedestrian in KITTI), some baselines actually improve. Additional experimental evidence is needed to reconcile this observation with the claimed advantage in sparsity.  
2.  **Ablation Studies on Feature Necessity:**  
    *   The necessity of combining *both* global and local features within the **template feature embedding module** lacks ablation validation. An ablation study isolating the contribution of each component is required.  
    *   Similarly, the design choice of using *both* global and local branches in the **shape-aware feature learning module** needs justification through ablation experiments demonstrating the contribution of each branch.  
3.  **Limited Scope of Ablation Studies:** The current ablation studies focus solely on the car category. Given that the method is evaluated on multiple object categories (cars, pedestrians, cyclists, trucks), the ablation studies must be expanded to include results across **all four categories** to ensure the findings are representative and generalizable.  
4.  **Clarity of Claims:** Some statements, such as "stable does not mean accurate," could be rephrased for greater clarity and precision.  
5.  **Limitations Discussion:** A brief discussion regarding the limitations of the BEV-based approach, particularly its potential unsuitability for complex indoor scenes where BEV maps may be insufficient for capturing vertical structures and occlusions, would strengthen the paper.

**Overall Assessment:**  
This paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound approach to 3D object tracking, achieving excellent results and making a valuable contribution to the field through its novel shape-aware feature learning and voxel-to-BEV localization. The writing is clear, and the methodology is well-described. While the experimental validation requires strengthening in the specific areas outlined above (particularly regarding claims on sparsity and the necessity of feature components, supported by broader ablation studies), the core strengths and the significant performance gains justify acceptance. Addressing the suggested improvements in a revision or future work will solidify the contributions further.

**Recommendation: 7: Good paper, accept**

The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: Novel 3D Object Tracker with Performance Improvements

**Overall Assessment:**
This paper presents a novel 3D object tracking method demonstrating significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art baselines while maintaining computational efficiency. The core technical contributions, particularly the use of point cloud generation during training, are interesting and yield strong results. However, significant issues with manuscript clarity, insufficient justification for certain design choices, and incomplete analysis currently detract from the work's impact. While the technical merits are sufficient for a marginal acceptance, substantial revisions addressing the weaknesses outlined below are essential before final publication. **I recommend acceptance conditional upon major revisions (Rating: 6 - Marginally above the acceptance threshold).**

**Major Strengths:**
1.  **Performance Gains:** The proposed method demonstrably outperforms preceding state-of-the-art methods by a noticeable and significant margin across the evaluated benchmarks. This is the paper's strongest asset.
2.  **Baselines and Partial Ablation:** The experimental setup includes appropriate baselines for comparison, and the authors have conducted partial ablation studies, providing some insight into the contributions of different components.
3.  **Computational Efficiency:** The thorough analysis of computational cost is a valuable contribution, clearly highlighting the method's efficiency and enhancing its potential for real-world deployment.
4.  **Innovative Training Technique:** The use of point cloud generation during training to improve feature expressiveness is an interesting and effective approach, contributing to the method's strong performance.

**Major Weaknesses:**
1.  **Writing Quality:** The manuscript suffers from poor writing quality, characterized by numerous grammatical errors, awkward phrasing, and unpolished sentences. This significantly hinders readability and comprehension, making it difficult to fully grasp the nuances of the method.
2.  **Insufficient Justification for Template Feature Embedding (TFE):** The TFE module appears heavily engineered, yet the paper lacks a detailed ablation study to rigorously justify its complexity and design choices. The theoretical underpinnings and the specific contributions of its components (e.g., global vs. local features, choice of distance functions) remain inadequately explored.
3.  **Lack of Novelty Attribution for Voxel-to-BEV:** The design of the Voxel-to-BEV localization component appears derivative of established methods in 3D object detection. Crucially, the paper fails to cite and properly position itself against foundational prior works like PointPillar and SECOND, creating a misleading impression of novelty for this specific module.
4.  **Inadequate Analysis of Limitations:** The paper lacks a thorough discussion of the method's limitations. Key challenges inherent to tracking, such as re-identification after occlusion and robust tracklet initialization, are not analyzed. Potential failure cases are not examined.
5.  **Missing Societal Impact Discussion:** The manuscript omits any discussion of the broader societal impacts, positive or negative, of the proposed tracking technology, which is a standard expectation.

**Recommendations for Revision:**
1.  **Proofreading and Language Editing:** The manuscript **must** undergo thorough proofreading by a native English speaker or professional editing service to correct all grammatical errors and significantly improve clarity, flow, and overall readability.
2.  **Comprehensive Ablation for TFE:** Conduct and report detailed ablation studies specifically for the Template Feature Embedding module. This should systematically evaluate:
    *   The individual contribution and necessity of global vs. local features.
    *   The impact of different distance/correlation functions used in generating similarity maps.
    *   The justification for the module's specific architectural complexity.
3.  **Proper Citation and Novelty Context for Voxel-to-BEV:** Explicitly acknowledge and cite the foundational works (PointPillar, SECOND) upon which the Voxel-to-BEV design is based. Clearly delineate the novelty of the *overall tracking framework* from this established component.
4.  **Analysis of Limitations and Failure Cases:** Add a dedicated section discussing the method's limitations. This must include analysis of challenges like re-identification, tracklet initialization, and any observed common failure modes in the experiments.
5.  **Societal Impact Discussion:** Include a brief section discussing potential societal impacts of the proposed tracking technology, considering both beneficial applications and potential ethical concerns or misuse scenarios.

**Conclusion:**
The proposed 3D object tracker demonstrates compelling performance improvements and computational efficiency, anchored by an interesting training technique. These technical merits justify marginal acceptance. However, publication is contingent upon the authors successfully addressing the significant weaknesses related to manuscript quality, insufficient justification for the TFE module, proper attribution for the Voxel-to-BEV design, and the addition of critical analysis regarding limitations and societal impact. Implementing the requested revisions will substantially strengthen the paper's clarity, rigor, and contribution.

Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review  

**Overall Assessment**  
This paper presents a compelling solution for 3D object tracking in sparse point clouds, demonstrating significant improvements on the KITTI benchmark. While the approach is innovative and well-executed, limitations in generalization and presentation clarity temper enthusiasm. The work is **marginally above the acceptance threshold (Rating: 6)**, and I recommend acceptance pending minor revisions.  

### Strengths  
1. **Problem Significance**: The paper tackles a highly challenging and relevant task in autonomous driving—robust 3D tracking under sparse point cloud conditions—addressing a critical gap in the field.  
2. **Algorithm Design**: The overall methodology is sensible, combining efficient feature extraction with motion-aware tracking. The design choices are well motivated and logically structured.  
3. **Strong Benchmark Performance**: Experimental results on KITTI show clear and significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods, validating the core technical contributions.  
4. **Presentation and Reproducibility**: The paper is generally well organized, and visualizations effectively illustrate design intuitions. The commitment to releasing code will greatly benefit reproducibility and future research.  

### Weaknesses and Concerns  
1. **Cyclist Tracking Deficiency**: The notably worse performance on the cyclist category (compared to cars/pedestrians) suggests limitations in handling smaller or irregularly shaped objects. This warrants deeper analysis.  
2. **Generalization Uncertainty**: Evaluation is limited to KITTI. Without cross-dataset validation or real-world deployment insights, the method’s robustness to new environments (e.g., adverse weather, denser traffic) remains unproven.  
3. **Implementation Ambiguities**:  
   - **Parameter Sensitivity**: Critical parameters (e.g., loss coefficients λ₁, λ₂, λ₃) require tuning without clear guidance. This could hinder adoption in new scenarios.  
   - **Visualization Issues**: Figure 3’s color scheme is ambiguous, reducing clarity. Video demonstrations of tracking outputs are also missing, limiting qualitative assessment.  
4. **Scope Constraints**: The approach processes only LiDAR data. Integration with RGB (i.e., multimodal inputs) remains unexplored despite its practical relevance.  

### Suggestions for Revision  
1. **Enhance Evaluation**:  
   - Analyze why cyclist tracking underperforms and propose mitigations (e.g., shape priors, dynamic modeling).  
   - Discuss generalization strategies for new datasets/sensors (e.g., nuScenes, Waymo) and provide tuning guidelines for loss coefficients.  
2. **Improve Presentation**:  
   - Clarify the color scheme in Figure 3 and include video supplements to showcase temporal tracking stability.  
   - Discuss broader safety implications for autonomous driving (e.g., collision avoidance in edge cases).  
3. **Future Work**: Briefly outline steps toward RGB+LiDAR fusion to enhance versatility.  

### Summary  
This work makes a valuable contribution to 3D object tracking with strong empirical results and a well-designed pipeline. However, concerns about generalization, cyclist performance, and reproducibility must be addressed to solidify its impact. With minor revisions to improve clarity and scope, the paper will be a welcome addition to the literature.  

**Decision**: **Accept (Rating: 6 - Marginally above acceptance threshold)**.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I didn't see specific discussions about the limitations and potential negative societal impact in the submission, please add these in the revised submission.

Main Review:

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. reviews.text:
## Peer Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a novel approach to single-object tracking in sparse point clouds, utilizing a Siamese network with tracking-by-detection. The core innovations include embedding template features into the search area via a shape completion loss and localizing targets through dense BEV (bird's-eye-view) anchor-free detection. Experimental results demonstrate significant performance improvements on the KITTI dataset compared to prior methods. While the technical approach is promising, the presentation requires substantial clarification, and the novelty claims and comparisons need stronger justification.  

**Strengths:**  
1. **Performance Improvement:** The proposed method achieves a significant boost in tracking performance, particularly on the challenging KITTI dataset with sparse point clouds. This is a valuable contribution to the field.  
2. **Effective Dense BEV Localization:** Replacing proposal-based localization (e.g., VoteNet in P2B) with dense anchor-free detection in BEV space proves advantageous for handling sparse objects, offering a robust alternative to existing frameworks.  

**Weaknesses and Major Concerns:**  
1. **Novelty Justification and Technical Comparison:**  
   - The relationship to foundational works P2B (framework) and SC3D (shape completion) is inadequately clarified. While the shape-aware feature learning module uses Chamfer distance (CD) loss for regularization—similar to SC3D—the claimed enhancements (gate mechanism for input features, global-local branch architecture) lack sufficient motivation and comparative analysis to convincingly demonstrate novelty over SC3D’s approach.  
   - The dense BEV detection component diverges from P2B’s proposal-based method but is presented without a clear technical comparison to justify its advantages beyond empirical results.  
2. **Methodology Presentation:** The method section is poorly structured and lacks clarity. Key architectural choices (e.g., gate mechanism, global-local branches) are inadequately motivated, making it difficult to follow the workflow and rationale.  
3. **Insufficient Experimental Analysis:**  
   - **Qualitative Results:** Visual comparisons with SC3D and P2B, especially for challenging categories like cyclists, are absent, limiting insight into failure modes and practical advantages.  
   - **Ablation Studies:** Critical modifications to SC3D’s shape completion loss (e.g., CD loss integration, input feature enhancements) lack targeted ablation analysis to validate their individual contributions.  
   - **Speed Discrepancy:** The reported speed (17 FPS on TITAN RTX) is notably slower than P2B’s 40 FPS (on GTX 1080Ti). This significant gap requires justification, as computational efficiency is crucial for real-world applications.  
4. **Missing Discussions:** Limitations of the approach (e.g., sensitivity to point cloud sparsity, scalability) and societal impact considerations are omitted.  

**Recommendations for Improvement:**  
1. **Clarify Novelty and Comparisons:**  
   - Explicitly differentiate the technical contributions of the shape-aware module from SC3D (e.g., via architectural diagrams or feature-space analysis).  
   - Discuss the framework’s relationship to P2B, highlighting how the dense BEV strategy addresses limitations of proposal-based tracking.  
2. **Revise Method Section:** Restructure for clarity, emphasizing the motivation behind each component (e.g., gate mechanism, BEV detection) and their integration into the overall pipeline.  
3. **Enhance Experiments:**  
   - Include qualitative results across all categories (especially cyclists), comparing failure cases and successes against SC3D/P2B.  
   - Conduct an ablation study isolating the impact of the shape completion modifications (e.g., CD loss, feature enhancements).  
   - Justify the speed discrepancy with P2B (e.g., via complexity analysis or hardware comparison).  
4. **Add Discussions:** Address limitations (e.g., performance drop in extreme sparsity) and potential negative societal impacts (e.g., misuse in surveillance).  

**Decision:**  
**Rating: 6 — Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
The paper presents a solid technical contribution with demonstrable gains on KITTI, particularly through its dense BEV detection paradigm. However, the insufficient justification of novelty, unclear comparisons with prior work, and lack of thorough experimental analysis weaken its impact. The manuscript requires major revisions—particularly in presentation, novelty framing, and experimental rigor—to meet publication standards. **Acceptance is recommended only if the authors convincingly address the above concerns in a rebuttal and subsequent revision.**

The results  look promising.

My major concern if novelty is limited.  The proposed method is an ensemble of well-known components: template matching (eg. SiamRPN[1]), shape-completion and bev localization (e.g. LaserNet[2]).

Ablation studies of global and local template features are desired.

[2]Meyer, Gregory P., et al. "Lasernet: An efficient probabilistic 3d object detector for autonomous driving." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2019.

Summary:
this paper proposed a Siamese voxel-to BEV tracker, including global and local template matching, shape-completion network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network.

Main Review:
The results  look promising.

My major concern if novelty is limited.  The proposed method is an ensemble of well-known components: template matching (eg. SiamRPN[1]), shape-completion and bev localization (e.g. LaserNet[2]).

Ablation studies of global and local template features are desired.

[1]Li, Bo, et al. "High performance visual tracking with siamese region proposal network." Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2018.
[2]Meyer, Gregory P., et al. "Lasernet: An efficient probabilistic 3d object detector for autonomous driving." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2019.

yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper presents an approach combining template matching, shape completion, and bird's-eye view (BEV) localization for 3D object detection. While the experimental results demonstrate promising performance, I have reservations about the technical novelty and thoroughness of validation. My assessment is that the work meets the threshold for acceptance but requires substantial revisions to strengthen its contributions.

**Strengths**:  
The empirical results across multiple benchmarks suggest the proposed method achieves competitive detection accuracy. The integration of complementary components (template matching and BEV features) appears beneficial, demonstrating practical value for 3D perception tasks.

**Weaknesses**:  
The technical approach largely combines established methodologies: template matching via SiamRPN-style mechanisms, BEV processing reminiscent of LaserNet, and shape completion techniques. While the integration is competently executed, the paper does not sufficiently differentiate its core contributions from prior ensemble methods. Specifically:  
1. Limited novelty in component selection, with no fundamental algorithmic innovations beyond the combination strategy  
2. Insufficient ablation analyses (e.g., no controlled experiments isolating the impact of global vs. local template features)  

**Suggestions for Improvement**:  
1. Conduct systematic ablation studies to quantify how each proposed component (particularly the dual-template feature embedding) contributes to final performance  
2. Clarify the novel aspects of the integration methodology beyond straightforward implementation stitching  
3. Strengthen the related work section by explicitly contrasting with other multi-component detection frameworks  

**Technical Concerns**:  
The reliance on SiamRPN-derived template matching (Section 3.2) and LaserNet-inspired BEV processing (Section 3.4) risks positioning the work as an incremental combination rather than a conceptual advance. The claimed benefits of joint template-BEV reasoning require more rigorous validation through component-level ablations.

**Decision**:  
I recommend **acceptance** (Rating 6 - Marginally above threshold) based on the empirical results and practical implementation insights, but suggest major revisions to better articulate the methodological contributions relative to existing component-based approaches. The current version makes a borderline case that could be strengthened through the requested technical validations.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines
reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Point Cloud-Based 3D Single-Object Tracking with Shape Completion"

**Overall Summary:**
This paper presents a novel approach for 3D single-object tracking using point clouds, specifically leveraging shape completion of the search area to enhance performance under sparse point cloud conditions. The method demonstrates intuitive engineering, particularly in feature embedding, and shows qualitative improvements on the KITTI dataset. While the core idea is promising and the results indicate potential, significant concerns remain regarding the validation of the primary contributions and the robustness of the evaluation. These limitations currently prevent a stronger endorsement, though the work is deemed marginally above the acceptance threshold.

**Strengths:**
1.  **Intuitive Feature Engineering:** The proposed method for local and global feature embedding within the template feature extraction module is well-conceived and represents a clear strength. This design choice effectively supports the tracking task.
2.  **Qualitative Demonstration:** The qualitative analysis successfully illustrates the utility of the shape completion approach, particularly in scenarios involving sparse point clouds, highlighting the potential practical value of the method.

**Weaknesses and Major Concerns:**
The primary weaknesses center on insufficient validation of the core contributions and limitations in the evaluation protocol:
1.  **Lack of Rigorous Contribution Validation:**
    *   **Shape Completion Contribution:** The paper claims shape completion of the search area as a key innovation. However, there is no direct, controlled comparison against the most relevant prior work, specifically the template-based shape completion approach presented in [13]. Demonstrating superiority or clear advantages over [13] under identical conditions (e.g., same backbone, training data) is crucial to isolate and validate this contribution. The current comparisons, involving different codebases, do not convincingly isolate the impact of the shape completion module itself.
    *   **Voxel-to-BEV Contribution:** The proposed voxel-to-BEV localization module, especially its mechanism for z-axis center regression (max-pooling along the z-axis followed by MLP), lacks sufficient justification and empirical validation. The design choice appears counter-intuitive, and no ablation study is provided to quantify its impact on both 2D (x,y) and z-axis estimation performance compared to alternative strategies. The paper fails to demonstrate that this specific design is necessary or optimal.
2.  **Inadequate Network Design Justification:** The choice of PointNet++ as the backbone network requires stronger justification. PointNet++ is a relatively older architecture, and numerous more advanced point cloud processing methods have since been developed. The authors should either provide a compelling rationale for selecting PointNet++ over more recent alternatives or consider integrating a more state-of-the-art backbone to ensure the method's competitiveness and longevity.
3.  **Limited and Non-Robust Evaluation:**
    *   The evaluation is restricted to only two sequences from the KITTI dataset. This severely limits the statistical significance of the results and raises concerns about the generalizability of the findings. Performance on such a small subset may not be representative.
    *   Furthermore, the evaluation suffers from insufficient samples for specific object categories like Van and Cyclist within these limited sequences, further undermining the reliability of the category-specific results and overall conclusions.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**
To strengthen the paper and address the major concerns, the authors should consider the following revisions:
1.  **Conduct a direct, controlled comparison** between the proposed shape completion approach and the method from [13], ensuring identical experimental conditions (backbone, training data, etc.) to isolate and validate the contribution of the novel shape completion component.
2.  **Perform comprehensive ablation studies** for the voxel-to-BEV module, clearly demonstrating its individual contribution to the tracking performance, specifically isolating its impact on 2D (x,y) center estimation and z-axis regression. Justify the chosen z-axis regression mechanism with empirical evidence.
3.  **Justify the use of PointNet++** in detail, especially in light of more recent point cloud architectures, or **adopt a more advanced backbone network** and demonstrate its impact.
4.  **Significantly expand the evaluation** to include larger, more diverse, and challenging datasets such as nuScenes or Waymo. This is essential to demonstrate the robustness, generalizability, and statistical significance of the results beyond the minimal KITTI subset used.

**Overall Recommendation:**
While the core idea of using shape completion for sparse point cloud tracking is interesting and the qualitative results on KITTI are promising, the significant reservations regarding the validation of the primary contributions (shape completion and voxel-to-BEV design) and the limited evaluation protocol prevent a stronger recommendation. The paper demonstrates potential and is marginally above the acceptance threshold. **I recommend acceptance, contingent upon the authors addressing the major concerns raised above, particularly regarding the validation of contributions and evaluation robustness.** The revisions suggested are essential to solidify the claims and demonstrate the true value and novelty of the proposed method.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

Main Review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
