reviews.summary: The paper proposes the use of RL for instance segmentation. In particular, the paper uses super pixels together with graph partitioning and RL to learn the labels of instances. The model is validated on two datasets, one synthetic and one biomedical imaging dataset and is numerically compared to two approaches ([28] and [47]).
reviews.main_review:
**Originality:**

In the paper, the main motivation of the use of RL is to enable optimization of discrete objective. However, this use of RL is standard. Moreover, given this paper motivations, the reviewer would expect to see empirical comparisons to alternative methods to learn discrete assignments  (e.g. straight through, DETR-style https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872, [8]). Thus, from the methodological point of view the novelty is rather limited. From the application point of view, the instance segmentation of light microscopy images, the paper might have some edge of originality; however, it is hard to assess it given the niche domain. To fully appreciate the originality of the method it would be beneficial to validate the approach on a more standard benchmark. 


**Clarity:**

The paper is generally clearly written. However, the clarity of the methods could be improved. The presentation of this section is a bit convoluted making it hard to grasp the overall picture of the proposed approach. The reviewer would encourage the authors to simplify the presentation of the Methods, especially focusing on the aspects of why particular decisions were made. E.g. why are the authors considering method 1 and 2? Why stateless RL with sequence length of 1? Why not use straight through?

For a non-microscopy expert, it is hard to understand the color codings of Figs 4 and 5. 


**Quality and significance:**

The quality and significance of the results are hard to assess given the niche application domain. It would be beneficial to test the ideas on some more standard benchmarks to instance image segmentation (e.g. COCO). 

Additional questions comments:
- Why are superpixels a good prior for this task? 
- Would it be possible to use other priors/partitioning methods?
- It would be nice to discuss the tradeoffs between added computational costs due to the use of RL and metric gains.
- Would it be possible to report the numbers of a standard instance segmentation model for this task, (e.g. https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06870 or some followup)?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: There is no limitation section/paragraph nor societal impact section/paragraph. Please add those to the conclusions section.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:

The paper proposes the use of RL for instance segmentation. In particular, the paper uses super pixels together with graph partitioning and RL to learn the labels of instances. The model is validated on two datasets, one synthetic and one biomedical imaging dataset and is numerically compared to two approaches ([28] and [47]).

Main Review:
**Originality:**

In the paper, the main motivation of the use of RL is to enable optimization of discrete objective. However, this use of RL is standard. Moreover, given this paper motivations, the reviewer would expect to see empirical comparisons to alternative methods to learn discrete assignments  (e.g. straight through, DETR-style https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872, [8]). Thus, from the methodological point of view the novelty is rather limited. From the application point of view, the instance segmentation of light microscopy images, the paper might have some edge of originality; however, it is hard to assess it given the niche domain. To fully appreciate the originality of the method it would be beneficial to validate the approach on a more standard benchmark. 


**Clarity:**

The paper is generally clearly written. However, the clarity of the methods could be improved. The presentation of this section is a bit convoluted making it hard to grasp the overall picture of the proposed approach. The reviewer would encourage the authors to simplify the presentation of the Methods, especially focusing on the aspects of why particular decisions were made. E.g. why are the authors considering method 1 and 2? Why stateless RL with sequence length of 1? Why not use straight through?

For a non-microscopy expert, it is hard to understand the color codings of Figs 4 and 5. 


**Quality and significance:**

The quality and significance of the results are hard to assess given the niche application domain. It would be beneficial to test the ideas on some more standard benchmarks to instance image segmentation (e.g. COCO). 

Additional questions comments:
- Why are superpixels a good prior for this task? 
- Would it be possible to use other priors/partitioning methods?
- It would be nice to discuss the tradeoffs between added computational costs due to the use of RL and metric gains.
- Would it be possible to report the numbers of a standard instance segmentation model for this task, (e.g. https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06870 or some followup)?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
There is no limitation section/paragraph nor societal impact section/paragraph. Please add those to the conclusions section.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Reinforcement Learning with Superpixels and Graph Partitioning for Instance Segmentation"

**Summary of the Paper and Review:**  
This paper presents an approach using reinforcement learning (RL) combined with superpixel priors and graph partitioning for instance segmentation, evaluated on synthetic and biomedical microscopy datasets. While the application domain is specific, the work suffers from significant limitations regarding novelty, methodological justification, and evaluation. Consequently, I recommend rejection.

**Strengths:**  
*   The paper is generally well-structured and clearly written.
*   There is some potential originality in applying this specific RL-based segmentation approach to biomedical microscopy data, although the significance of this contribution is difficult to gauge definitively within the niche domain.

**Major Weaknesses and Concerns:**  
*   **Limited Methodological Novelty:** The core idea of employing RL for discrete decision-making tasks (like segmentation assignments) is well-established in the literature. The paper fails to sufficiently differentiate its specific RL formulation or demonstrate a significant advancement over existing RL strategies for similar problems.
*   **Inadequate Methodological Comparisons:** A critical flaw is the absence of comparisons to relevant alternative methods for learning discrete assignments. Key approaches like straight-through gradient estimation techniques or DETR-style architectures (e.g., reference [8]) are not benchmarked against. This omission makes it impossible to assess whether the proposed RL approach offers tangible benefits over established or simpler techniques.
*   **Significance Difficult to Assess:** The exclusive focus on a highly specialized biomedical microscopy application, without validation on standard computer vision benchmarks (e.g., COCO), hinders the broader assessment of the method's significance, robustness, and general applicability. The performance gains observed in the niche domain lack context without comparison to widely recognized baselines like Mask R-CNN.
*   **Lack of Critical Sections:** The paper omits essential discussions on limitations and societal impact. Addressing potential biases, failure modes, computational burdens, and broader implications is crucial for a complete manuscript.
*   **Clarity and Presentation Issues:** The Methods section is overly complex and difficult to follow. Key technical aspects lack clear justification:
    *   The fundamental rationale for using superpixels as priors is underdeveloped.
    *   The choice of a *stateless* RL approach with an episode length of 1 requires much stronger motivation.
    *   The distinction between Method 1 (end-to-end feature extractor training) and Method 2 (fixed pre-trained features) lacks a clear, well-argued motivation.
*   **Unanalyzed Trade-offs:** The computational costs inherent in the RL implementation are not discussed, nor are they weighed against the reported metric improvements. This cost-benefit analysis is vital for practical adoption.
*   **Figure Clarity:** The color codings in Figures 4 and 5 are not sufficiently explained for readers unfamiliar with the specific biomedical context, reducing their effectiveness.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
Should the authors consider a significant revision or future work, the following are essential:
1.  **Add Comprehensive Sections:** Include a detailed Limitations section and a Societal Impact statement.
2.  **Conduct Essential Comparisons:** Benchmark the method rigorously against relevant alternatives (straight-through, DETR-style, [8]) and report results from standard models (e.g., Mask R-CNN) on the same datasets.
3.  **Validate on Standard Benchmarks:** Demonstrate the method's performance and generalizability on a widely accepted dataset like COCO.
4.  **Improve Methodological Clarity and Justification:** Significantly revise the Methods section for clarity. Explicitly justify key design choices: the use of superpixels, the stateless RL formulation with episode length 1, and the rationale behind comparing Method 1 and Method 2.
5.  **Analyze Computational Trade-offs:** Explicitly discuss the computational overhead of the RL approach and analyze whether the performance gains justify this cost.
6.  **Enhance Figure Accessibility:** Improve the explanation of color schemes in Figures 4 and 5 for a broader audience.

**Decision:**  
**4: Ok but not good enough - Rejection**  
The paper addresses an interesting application but presents a method lacking significant novelty over established RL techniques for discrete tasks. The absence of crucial comparisons to alternative methods, the lack of validation on standard benchmarks, the difficulty in assessing broader significance due to the niche domain, the convoluted methodology description, and the omission of limitations and societal impact sections collectively constitute major shortcomings that preclude acceptance in the current form. The weaknesses substantially outweigh the strengths.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes the use of RL for instance segmentation. In particular, the paper uses super pixels together with graph partitioning and RL to learn the labels of instances. The model is validated on two datasets, one synthetic and one biomedical imaging dataset and is numerically compared to two approaches ([28] and [47]).

Main Review:
**Originality:**

In the paper, the main motivation of the use of RL is to enable optimization of discrete objective. However, this use of RL is standard. Moreover, given this paper motivations, the reviewer would expect to see empirical comparisons to alternative methods to learn discrete assignments  (e.g. straight through, DETR-style https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872, [8]). Thus, from the methodological point of view the novelty is rather limited. From the application point of view, the instance segmentation of light microscopy images, the paper might have some edge of originality; however, it is hard to assess it given the niche domain. To fully appreciate the originality of the method it would be beneficial to validate the approach on a more standard benchmark. 


**Clarity:**

The paper is generally clearly written. However, the clarity of the methods could be improved. The presentation of this section is a bit convoluted making it hard to grasp the overall picture of the proposed approach. The reviewer would encourage the authors to simplify the presentation of the Methods, especially focusing on the aspects of why particular decisions were made. E.g. why are the authors considering method 1 and 2? Why stateless RL with sequence length of 1? Why not use straight through?

For a non-microscopy expert, it is hard to understand the color codings of Figs 4 and 5. 


**Quality and significance:**

The quality and significance of the results are hard to assess given the niche application domain. It would be beneficial to test the ideas on some more standard benchmarks to instance image segmentation (e.g. COCO). 

Additional questions comments:
- Why are superpixels a good prior for this task? 
- Would it be possible to use other priors/partitioning methods?
- It would be nice to discuss the tradeoffs between added computational costs due to the use of RL and metric gains.
- Would it be possible to report the numbers of a standard instance segmentation model for this task, (e.g. https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06870 or some followup)?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
There is no limitation section/paragraph nor societal impact section/paragraph. Please add those to the conclusions section.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: Finite-Time Error Bounds for Distributed Linear Stochastic Approximation

reviews.summary: An interesting submission dealing with distributed stochastic approximation driven by Markovian noise, in which the communication topologies considered among agents are captured by a stochastic matrix (in contrast to the doubly stochastic matrix studied in the related literature). Both asymptotic and finite-time error bounds are established and it was shown that the algorithm converges to some unspecified convex combination of the equilibrium points of local tasks. This paper finally proposes a push-type distributed stochastic approximation algorithm and provides its finite-time bounds for the performance by leveraging the analysis for the consensus-type algorithm with stochastic matrices. All analysis and error bounds are directly applicable to distributed TD algorithms.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: This paper studies distributed Markovian stochastic approximation algorithms with stochastic interconnection matrices, which has not yet been covered in the related literature. The results are a bit different than what is known for related algorithms having doubly stochastic interconnection matrices. Further, a push-type algorithm is proposed for decentralized implementation. 

Quality. The results are technically sound and theoretically supported. The algorithm proposed is also new.

Clarity. The paper is well written and easy to follow. The results are nicely organized and the contributions relative to existing works are clear too.

Significance. The results are important and complement the existing distributed Markovian stochastic approximation results which have only focused on doubly stochastic interconnections among agents. The tools developed for analysis may also be useful for further use.

Summary:
An interesting submission dealing with distributed stochastic approximation driven by Markovian noise, in which the communication topologies considered among agents are captured by a stochastic matrix (in contrast to the doubly stochastic matrix studied in the related literature). Both asymptotic and finite-time error bounds are established and it was shown that the algorithm converges to some unspecified convex combination of the equilibrium points of local tasks. This paper finally proposes a push-type distributed stochastic approximation algorithm and provides its finite-time bounds for the performance by leveraging the analysis for the consensus-type algorithm with stochastic matrices. All analysis and error bounds are directly applicable to distributed TD algorithms.

Main Review:
Originality: This paper studies distributed Markovian stochastic approximation algorithms with stochastic interconnection matrices, which has not yet been covered in the related literature. The results are a bit different than what is known for related algorithms having doubly stochastic interconnection matrices. Further, a push-type algorithm is proposed for decentralized implementation. 

Quality. The results are technically sound and theoretically supported. The algorithm proposed is also new.

Clarity. The paper is well written and easy to follow. The results are nicely organized and the contributions relative to existing works are clear too.

Significance. The results are important and complement the existing distributed Markovian stochastic approximation results which have only focused on doubly stochastic interconnections among agents. The tools developed for analysis may also be useful for further use.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Numerical simulations may be provided comparing the proposed algorithm and existing ones as well as validating the proved finite-time error bounds for both constant/time-varying stepsizes.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
**Review**  
This paper makes significant theoretical contributions to distributed stochastic approximation by relaxing the conventional doubly stochastic communication matrix assumption. The work extends existing analyses to more practical stochastic matrices while establishing both asymptotic and non-asymptotic convergence guarantees. While some implementation-focused aspects could be strengthened, the methodological advances and rigorous analysis warrant acceptance.

**Strengths**:  
1. **Novel Problem Setting**: The relaxation to stochastic (non-doubly-stochastic) communication matrices addresses a critical limitation in prior work, enabling analysis of uni-directional communication protocols. This expands the applicability to realistic distributed systems.  
2. **Technical Rigor**: The paper provides a comprehensive theoretical framework, including asymptotic convergence proofs and novel finite-time error bounds for both constant and diminishing step-sizes. The push-type algorithm derivation for column-stochastic matrices is particularly noteworthy.  
3. **Analytical Tools**: The use of quadratic Lyapunov comparison functions with absolute probability sequences offers a fresh perspective that could inspire future extensions. The consensus error decomposition strategy elegantly separates network effects from local approximation errors.  
4. **Clarity of Contributions**: The paper clearly delineates its advances over prior work, particularly in handling time-varying graphs and non-Markovian noise via geometric mixing assumptions.  

**Weaknesses**:  
1. **Empirical Validation**: The absence of numerical simulations leaves open questions about the tightness of derived bounds and practical convergence rates. Comparisons with doubly-stochastic baselines would strengthen impact.  
2. **Interpretability of Constants**: The finite-time bounds contain complex constants (e.g., dependent on spectral gaps) that obscure actionable insights for parameter tuning.  
3. **Implementation Considerations**: There is no discussion of computational/memory costs, which matters for resource-constrained edge applications.  
4. **Limited Push-Sum Analysis**: The push-sum variant analysis focuses solely on time-varying step-sizes without addressing constant step-size regimes.  

**Suggestions for Improvement**:  
1. **Experimental Validation**: Include simulations comparing convergence rates against doubly-stochastic methods under varying network topologies.  
2. **Constant Simplification**: Explore whether dependencies in Theorem bounds can be summarized via problem-specific parameters (e.g., graph diameter).  
3. **Application Studies**: Demonstrate the push-sum algorithm in multi-agent reinforcement learning scenarios, as suggested by the distributed TD learning connection.  
4. **Extended Analysis**: Investigate constant step-size regimes for push-sum protocols to complement the current diminishing step-size results.  

**Main Contributions**:  
- First finite-time analysis for stochastic approximation with time-varying stochastic matrices, removing the restrictive doubly-stochastic requirement.  
- Introduction of a push-sum protocol variant that recovers average equilibrium under team objectives in uni-directional networks.  
- Novel analytical framework bridging consensus theory tools (absolute probability sequences) with stochastic approximation error propagation.  

**Conclusion**:  
This work provides foundational theoretical advances in distributed stochastic approximation, with implications for decentralized optimization and learning. While practical implementation aspects require further exploration, the technical depth and methodological innovations justify acceptance. The analysis tools and problem formulation will likely stimulate productive follow-up research in both control and learning communities.  

**Decision**: 7: Good paper, accept

reviews.text:
## Peer Review  

**Overall Assessment:**  
This paper presents valuable contributions to the analysis of distributed linear stochastic approximation (SA) algorithms, specifically relaxing the conventional doubly stochastic assumption for interconnection matrices and introducing a Push-SA algorithm for directed graphs. The theoretical analysis of finite-time error bounds under stochastic matrices is rigorous and well-motivated. However, while the technical contributions are clear and the exposition is generally strong, the work lacks sufficient contextual motivation regarding the application of SA in reinforcement learning (RL). This omission limits the paper’s impact for a broader audience. I recommend **rejection in its current form** (marginally below the acceptance threshold), as the addition of this context would significantly strengthen the manuscript.  

**Summary of Contributions:**  
The authors address two key challenges in distributed SA:  
1.  **Relaxed Matrix Assumptions:** Providing finite-time error analysis for distributed linear SA under *stochastic* (not necessarily doubly stochastic) interconnection matrices, enhancing the generality and applicability of prior work.  
2.  **Algorithm for Directed Graphs:** Introducing and analyzing a Push-SA algorithm based on the push-sum approach, enabling operation over directed interaction graphs.  

These contributions are clearly explained, and the comparative discussion with existing works effectively highlights the advancements.  

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Generality of Analysis:** The relaxation of the doubly stochastic matrix assumption to the more practical and general case of stochastic matrices is a significant and valuable contribution, broadening the scope of applicable scenarios.  
2.  **Clarity of Exposition:** The assumptions, theoretical results, and their relation to prior literature are presented clearly and coherently, facilitating understanding of the technical advancements.  
3.  **Novel Algorithm:** The proposal and analysis of the Push-SA algorithm for directed graphs address an important practical constraint in distributed systems.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1.  **Insufficient Contextual Motivation:** The paper lacks a substantive discussion on the critical role of stochastic approximation (SA) in reinforcement learning (RL), particularly concerning temporal difference (TD) learning. This omission weakens the motivation for readers from the RL community and fails to fully situate the technical contributions within a major application domain. Providing this context is essential for demonstrating the broader impact and relevance of the presented analysis.  

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1.  **Enhance Motivation:** Add a brief but clear discussion (e.g., in the introduction or related work) explicitly connecting distributed SA to its fundamental role in RL algorithms like TD learning. Explain how the challenges addressed (stochastic matrices, directed graphs) are relevant in RL settings (e.g., multi-agent RL with decentralized communication). This will significantly strengthen the paper's motivation and impact.  
2.  **Minor Corrections:**  
    *   Line 36: Replace "arguably" with "**inarguably**".  
    *   Line 37: Replace "differing" with "**different**".  

**Conclusion:**  
The technical core of the paper – the finite-time error analysis under relaxed matrix assumptions and the novel Push-SA algorithm – is sound and constitutes a valuable contribution to the theory of distributed optimization. The clarity of the exposition is commendable. However, the lack of sufficient context regarding the pivotal application of SA in RL significantly diminishes the perceived impact and motivation for a key segment of the potential audience. Addressing this motivational gap is crucial for elevating the paper to the acceptance threshold. I encourage the authors to revise the manuscript accordingly.

reviews.summary: This paper studies a distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm. The interconnection structure among the agents is described by a time varying directed graph. Under the setting of Markovian samples, the main contribution is to characterize the finite time error bound on the mean squared error. The analysis is applied to study a push-sum version of distributed linear stochastic approximation.
reviews.main_review:
As mentioned, the main contribution is a new analysis on the distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm given in (1), encompassing constant stepsize and time varying step size, Markovian samples. To achieve this, the study introduces a technique known as the absolute probability sequence to track the evolution of error in light of the time varying directed graph which results in a row-stochastic interconnection matrix. This technique can be interesting as it leads to a simple recursion of the iterates in (4), simplifying the subsequent analysis.

However, there are a number of drawbacks which outweigh the benefits of the current paper, as discussed below:

- Though the paper title reads "distributed linear stochastic approximation", the algorithm in (1) only considers a restricted setting with an identical driving matrix $A(x)$ across agents (and different $b^i(x)$ across agents). This setting may be suitable for the distributed policy evaluation problem where a number of prior works have appeared, but it does not include many interesting cases for distributed linear stochastic approximation including classical ones such as linear regression. In this regard, the paper may better position itself as a paper for distributed policy evaluation insteado of the more general linear stochastic approximation. 

- The main theorems (Theorem 2 & 3) require Assumption 6 which is not well justified. Particularly, this assumption requires the absolute probability seqeunce to admit a limit vector. The Remark 3 which follows the assumption fails to provide a convincing case for this assumption to hold. Therein, two examples are given: the case $W_t$ is doubly stochastic is rather trivial as it defies one of the main relaxation considered in this paper; the case when the number of neighbors of agents are unchanged over time (also, is it the number of out-neighbors? or in-neighbors?) appears to be too artificial. The paper shall include a more concrete construction on how the assumption holds. 

- In addition, it is not clear what are the benefits of employing the absolute probability sequence technique in analyzing the convergence of distributed algorithms, e.g., when compared to the classical approach in [52] for the push-SA algorithm. As for the convergence to a weighted average of the equilibrium point when a row-stochastic mixing matrix is employed, some relevant work can be found in the static graph setting, e.g.,

-- J. Chen, A. Sayed, "Distributed Pareto Optimization via Diffusion Strategies", IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing, 2013. 

- The presentation of the main theorems lacks useful information that a finite-time error analysis should encompass. For instance, it is mentioned that "$C_2$ depends on $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$ ..." which does not describe how small/large the bound is - in fact, without a more detailed description, the bound in (7) is not useful since $C_2$ can be large; the result for push-SA is summarized in Theorem 5 *only* for the case of time varying step size, and it is mentioned that "our current analysis approach cannot be directly applied for this case" which lacks further expatiation; in theorem 3, how does that the convergence rate of $\eta_t$ affects the finite time error bound in the constant step size case? 

---
**Post Rebuttal**

The reviewer appreciates the detailed response from the authors. Unfortunately, the response has not addressed my previous concerns and therefore my previous evaluation stands. In particular, the promised "simulation based" study will not be very convincing since cases which violate Assumption 6 may still include a lot of cases. Instead, it would be better to construct an **analytical** example to illustrate the necessity of Assumption 6. In summary, my main concern is that Assumption 6 and its corresponding analysis due to the "absolute probability sequence" is one of the main innovations claimed in the paper, yet its applications are so far only limited to the previously known cases. 

Moreover, while it is acceptable that $C_2$ can be a large constant, it is also important to point out **how** does $C_2$ depends on the system parameter $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$. The current presentation has so far eluded from discussing about that.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: There isn't any potential negative societal impact. The limitation/suggestions for improvement have been discussed in the main review above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies a distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm. The interconnection structure among the agents is described by a time varying directed graph. Under the setting of Markovian samples, the main contribution is to characterize the finite time error bound on the mean squared error. The analysis is applied to study a push-sum version of distributed linear stochastic approximation.

Main Review:
As mentioned, the main contribution is a new analysis on the distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm given in (1), encompassing constant stepsize and time varying step size, Markovian samples. To achieve this, the study introduces a technique known as the absolute probability sequence to track the evolution of error in light of the time varying directed graph which results in a row-stochastic interconnection matrix. This technique can be interesting as it leads to a simple recursion of the iterates in (4), simplifying the subsequent analysis.

However, there are a number of drawbacks which outweigh the benefits of the current paper, as discussed below:

- Though the paper title reads "distributed linear stochastic approximation", the algorithm in (1) only considers a restricted setting with an identical driving matrix $A(x)$ across agents (and different $b^i(x)$ across agents). This setting may be suitable for the distributed policy evaluation problem where a number of prior works have appeared, but it does not include many interesting cases for distributed linear stochastic approximation including classical ones such as linear regression. In this regard, the paper may better position itself as a paper for distributed policy evaluation insteado of the more general linear stochastic approximation. 

- The main theorems (Theorem 2 & 3) require Assumption 6 which is not well justified. Particularly, this assumption requires the absolute probability seqeunce to admit a limit vector. The Remark 3 which follows the assumption fails to provide a convincing case for this assumption to hold. Therein, two examples are given: the case $W_t$ is doubly stochastic is rather trivial as it defies one of the main relaxation considered in this paper; the case when the number of neighbors of agents are unchanged over time (also, is it the number of out-neighbors? or in-neighbors?) appears to be too artificial. The paper shall include a more concrete construction on how the assumption holds. 

- In addition, it is not clear what are the benefits of employing the absolute probability sequence technique in analyzing the convergence of distributed algorithms, e.g., when compared to the classical approach in [52] for the push-SA algorithm. As for the convergence to a weighted average of the equilibrium point when a row-stochastic mixing matrix is employed, some relevant work can be found in the static graph setting, e.g.,

-- J. Chen, A. Sayed, "Distributed Pareto Optimization via Diffusion Strategies", IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing, 2013. 

- The presentation of the main theorems lacks useful information that a finite-time error analysis should encompass. For instance, it is mentioned that "$C_2$ depends on $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$ ..." which does not describe how small/large the bound is - in fact, without a more detailed description, the bound in (7) is not useful since $C_2$ can be large; the result for push-SA is summarized in Theorem 5 *only* for the case of time varying step size, and it is mentioned that "our current analysis approach cannot be directly applied for this case" which lacks further expatiation; in theorem 3, how does that the convergence rate of $\eta_t$ affects the finite time error bound in the constant step size case? 

---
**Post Rebuttal**

The reviewer appreciates the detailed response from the authors. Unfortunately, the response has not addressed my previous concerns and therefore my previous evaluation stands. In particular, the promised "simulation based" study will not be very convincing since cases which violate Assumption 6 may still include a lot of cases. Instead, it would be better to construct an **analytical** example to illustrate the necessity of Assumption 6. In summary, my main concern is that Assumption 6 and its corresponding analysis due to the "absolute probability sequence" is one of the main innovations claimed in the paper, yet its applications are so far only limited to the previously known cases. 

Moreover, while it is acceptable that $C_2$ can be a large constant, it is also important to point out **how** does $C_2$ depends on the system parameter $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$. The current presentation has so far eluded from discussing about that.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
There isn't any potential negative societal impact. The limitation/suggestions for improvement have been discussed in the main review above.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review  

**Overall Assessment**  
This paper presents a novel contribution by deriving finite-time error bounds for distributed linear stochastic approximation (SA) under time-varying directed graphs and Markovian sampling, leveraging absolute probability sequences to simplify the analysis. While the technical approach is interesting and addresses a relevant problem, significant limitations restrict the scope, justification, and practical utility of the work. The paper falls marginally below the acceptance threshold in its current form.  

**Strengths**  
1.  **Technical Innovation:** The use of absolute probability sequences to handle time-varying directed graphs is an interesting and potentially valuable methodological contribution. It offers a pathway to simplifying the analysis of distributed SA under non-trivial network dynamics.  
2.  **Problem Relevance:** Deriving finite-time error bounds for distributed linear SA with Markovian samples and time-varying directed graphs addresses a recognized gap in the literature and represents a meaningful step forward.  

**Weaknesses and Major Concerns**  
1.  **Overly Restricted Problem Setting:** The core analysis (Algorithm 1) assumes that the matrix `A(x)` is *identical* across all agents, differing only in the vector `b^i(x)`. This assumption excludes critical applications like distributed linear regression, where agents naturally possess distinct local data matrices (`A^i`). This significantly narrows the applicability of the claimed "general linear stochastic approximation" contribution. The paper would be more accurately framed as focusing on distributed *policy evaluation*.  
2.  **Unjustified Assumption (Assumption 6):** The critical Assumption 6, requiring the absolute probability sequence `{π_t}` to converge to some `π_∞`, lacks convincing justification. The authors provide no concrete analytical examples or sufficient discussion demonstrating that this assumption holds for a meaningful class of relevant sequences (e.g., sequences arising from common graph families or weight rules). This assumption underpins the main results but appears restrictive and inadequately motivated, limiting the perceived applicability of the theory.  
3.  **Unclear Advantages of Proposed Technique:** While the absolute probability sequence technique is presented as a simplifying tool, the paper fails to clearly articulate its advantages over existing methods for analyzing distributed SA under time-varying directed graphs (e.g., using push-sum directly or other consensus techniques). A direct comparison or discussion highlighting the specific benefits (e.g., simplicity, tighter bounds, broader applicability) is missing, making it difficult to assess the novelty's impact.  
4.  **Insufficiently Detailed Theoretical Bounds:** The practical utility of the derived bounds (e.g., Theorem 3) is hampered by insufficient detail regarding the constants. Specifically, while it is stated that constant `C₂` depends on parameters `L`, `γ_min`, `γ_max`, `A_max`, and `b_max`, the *exact nature* of this dependence (e.g., how `C₂` scales with `L` or the range of step-sizes) is not specified. This omission makes it difficult to interpret the bounds meaningfully or compare them rigorously with prior work.  
5.  **Limited Analysis Scope (Push-SA):** The analysis of the Push-SA algorithm (Algorithm 2) is only provided for the time-varying step-size case (Theorem 5). The constant step-size case, which is often of significant practical interest, is not analyzed, leaving a notable gap.  

**Suggestions for Improvement**  
To strengthen the paper and address the above concerns, the authors should consider the following revisions:  
1.  **Reframe the Contribution:** Reposition the paper's primary contribution around distributed policy evaluation, where the identical `A` assumption is more naturally justified, rather than claiming generality for linear SA. This provides a more accurate and compelling narrative.  
2.  **Justify Assumption 6:** Provide concrete analytical examples or proofs demonstrating that Assumption 6 holds for sequences generated by common classes of time-varying directed graphs (e.g., B-strongly connected sequences) or weight rules. Discuss the practical implications and limitations of this assumption more thoroughly.  
3.  **Clarify Advantages:** Include a dedicated discussion explicitly comparing the absolute probability sequence technique to existing approaches. Clearly state the benefits (e.g., analytical simplification, handling of directed graphs) and potential limitations compared to alternatives.  
4.  **Quantify Constant Dependencies:** Provide a detailed analysis or explicit expression for how key constants in the bounds (especially `C₂` in Theorem 3) scale with the parameters `L`, `γ_min`, `γ_max`, `A_max`, and `b_max`. This is crucial for understanding the tightness and practical implications of the results.  
5.  **Extend Push-SA Analysis:** Include a convergence rate analysis for the Push-SA algorithm (Algorithm 2) under a *constant* step-size regime (e.g., `η_t = η`). This would significantly enhance the paper's completeness and practical relevance.  
6.  **Refine Convergence Rate for Constant Step-Size:** For the constant step-size bounds (e.g., Theorem 3), explicitly include the convergence rate analysis for the step-size sequence `η_t` itself within the bound expression to enhance clarity.  

**Recommendation**  
The paper introduces a potentially useful technique and tackles an important problem. However, the combination of an overly restrictive problem setting, the lack of justification for a critical assumption (Assumption 6), insufficiently detailed theoretical bounds, and unclear advantages over existing methods significantly limits the paper's contribution and impact. Addressing the outlined weaknesses through substantial revisions, particularly reframing the scope, justifying assumptions, clarifying advantages, and detailing constants, is necessary. **Based on the current limitations, I recommend rejection. However, the core technical idea has merit, and I encourage the authors to address the concerns raised in a major revision.**  

**Rating:** 5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

Summary:
This paper studies a distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm. The interconnection structure among the agents is described by a time varying directed graph. Under the setting of Markovian samples, the main contribution is to characterize the finite time error bound on the mean squared error. The analysis is applied to study a push-sum version of distributed linear stochastic approximation.

Main Review:
As mentioned, the main contribution is a new analysis on the distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm given in (1), encompassing constant stepsize and time varying step size, Markovian samples. To achieve this, the study introduces a technique known as the absolute probability sequence to track the evolution of error in light of the time varying directed graph which results in a row-stochastic interconnection matrix. This technique can be interesting as it leads to a simple recursion of the iterates in (4), simplifying the subsequent analysis.

However, there are a number of drawbacks which outweigh the benefits of the current paper, as discussed below:

- Though the paper title reads "distributed linear stochastic approximation", the algorithm in (1) only considers a restricted setting with an identical driving matrix $A(x)$ across agents (and different $b^i(x)$ across agents). This setting may be suitable for the distributed policy evaluation problem where a number of prior works have appeared, but it does not include many interesting cases for distributed linear stochastic approximation including classical ones such as linear regression. In this regard, the paper may better position itself as a paper for distributed policy evaluation insteado of the more general linear stochastic approximation. 

- The main theorems (Theorem 2 & 3) require Assumption 6 which is not well justified. Particularly, this assumption requires the absolute probability seqeunce to admit a limit vector. The Remark 3 which follows the assumption fails to provide a convincing case for this assumption to hold. Therein, two examples are given: the case $W_t$ is doubly stochastic is rather trivial as it defies one of the main relaxation considered in this paper; the case when the number of neighbors of agents are unchanged over time (also, is it the number of out-neighbors? or in-neighbors?) appears to be too artificial. The paper shall include a more concrete construction on how the assumption holds. 

- In addition, it is not clear what are the benefits of employing the absolute probability sequence technique in analyzing the convergence of distributed algorithms, e.g., when compared to the classical approach in [52] for the push-SA algorithm. As for the convergence to a weighted average of the equilibrium point when a row-stochastic mixing matrix is employed, some relevant work can be found in the static graph setting, e.g.,

-- J. Chen, A. Sayed, "Distributed Pareto Optimization via Diffusion Strategies", IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing, 2013. 

- The presentation of the main theorems lacks useful information that a finite-time error analysis should encompass. For instance, it is mentioned that "$C_2$ depends on $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$ ..." which does not describe how small/large the bound is - in fact, without a more detailed description, the bound in (7) is not useful since $C_2$ can be large; the result for push-SA is summarized in Theorem 5 *only* for the case of time varying step size, and it is mentioned that "our current analysis approach cannot be directly applied for this case" which lacks further expatiation; in theorem 3, how does that the convergence rate of $\eta_t$ affects the finite time error bound in the constant step size case? 

---
**Post Rebuttal**

The reviewer appreciates the detailed response from the authors. Unfortunately, the response has not addressed my previous concerns and therefore my previous evaluation stands. In particular, the promised "simulation based" study will not be very convincing since cases which violate Assumption 6 may still include a lot of cases. Instead, it would be better to construct an **analytical** example to illustrate the necessity of Assumption 6. In summary, my main concern is that Assumption 6 and its corresponding analysis due to the "absolute probability sequence" is one of the main innovations claimed in the paper, yet its applications are so far only limited to the previously known cases. 

Moreover, while it is acceptable that $C_2$ can be a large constant, it is also important to point out **how** does $C_2$ depends on the system parameter $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$. The current presentation has so far eluded from discussing about that.

There isn't any potential negative societal impact. The limitation/suggestions for improvement have been discussed in the main review above.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review  

This paper presents a theoretical extension of distributed linear stochastic approximation (LSA) algorithms to time-varying directed graphs, offering a solid contribution to the field of decentralized optimization. While the technical core of the work is commendable, several significant limitations weaken its overall impact and practical assessment. Below, I outline the strengths, weaknesses, and suggestions for improvement.  

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Solid Theoretical Foundation:** The paper provides rigorous theoretical analysis, particularly in deriving convergence rates for both asymptotic and non-asymptotic cases. The mathematical derivations appear sound and technically sophisticated.  
2.  **Meaningful Extension:** Extending distributed LSA to the challenging setting of time-varying directed graphs is a valuable contribution, addressing a relevant gap in decentralized learning literature.  

**Weaknesses and Major Concerns:**  
1.  **Lack of Sample Complexity Analysis:** While convergence rates are derived, the paper omits a critical analysis of *sample complexity* (e.g., the total number of samples required across the network to achieve ε-accuracy). This omission makes it difficult to compare the efficiency of the proposed method fairly against state-of-the-art approaches, which often report such metrics.  
2.  **Absence of Empirical Validation:** The work relies solely on theoretical analysis. The absence of any empirical evaluation, even on simple toy problems, significantly undermines confidence in the practical utility, scalability, and robustness of the proposed algorithm. Empirical verification is essential to complement the theory.  
3.  **Unclear Impact of Problem Parameters:** The convergence rates depend on constants involving conditional numbers (e.g., related to the graph or the objective function). The paper notes this dependence but fails to provide a thorough analysis or discussion of *how* these conditional numbers quantitatively impact the convergence rates in practice. This lack of clarity limits the interpretability and practical guidance offered by the theoretical results.  

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1.  **Include Sample Complexity:** Derive and clearly state the sample complexity (e.g., total samples for ε-accuracy) to enable meaningful comparisons with existing decentralized stochastic approximation methods.  
2.  **Add Empirical Validation:** Incorporate a section with numerical experiments on benchmark problems (even simple linear regression tasks). This should demonstrate the algorithm's performance, validate the theoretical convergence rates, and illustrate sensitivity to parameters like network size or conditioning.  
3.  **Analyze Parameter Dependence:** Provide a detailed discussion and, if possible, illustrative examples or analysis quantifying how the convergence rate constants (particularly those involving conditional numbers) affect the actual convergence speed. This is crucial for understanding the algorithm's behavior in real-world scenarios.  

**Overall Assessment:**  
The paper makes a noteworthy theoretical contribution by extending distributed LSA to time-varying directed networks. The technical analysis of convergence rates is solid. However, the absence of sample complexity results, the complete lack of empirical verification, and the insufficient discussion of the impact of conditional numbers on convergence significantly diminish the work's practical relevance and completeness. These shortcomings place the paper just below the threshold for acceptance in its current form.  

**Decision:**  
**5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold**  
**Recommendation:** Reject

title: Local $K$-means: An Efficient Optimization Algorithm And Its Generalization

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a local efficient version of k-means which leverages the KNN graph of the data to compute neighborhoods of data. Extensive empirical results both on synthetic and real data are reported.
reviews.main_review:
Running times are not reported for the results on real data in Table 3. One of the main advantages of the proposed method is gained efficiency with respect to the clustering competitors, therefore running times should be reported and discussed. 

The proposed method LKM also depends on the initialization of the clustering y, and this is not discussed. It's unclear whether the results on simulated data in Table 1 are averages of different runs or not. This is not discussed and it should. Results should be averages and standard deviations should also be reported.

It is well-known that k-means is sensitive to outliers, but it's unclear in Figure 3(b) why the performance of k-means is poor in this case. It seems it was still able to detect the four clusters in the data?

In Section 3, the definition of neighborhood of a point is confusing and not necessarily correct. The notion of neighbor is not symmetric: x_j may be a neighbor of x_i, but not vice versa. 

I don't find the analogy of KNN graph with social networks particularly useful or insightful. They are not quite the same concept, really. The paper can instead be improved by discussing related work on exemplar-based approaches aimed at making clustering more robust. See for example recent work on regularized NMF methods which leverage the KNN graphs of the data, e.g. Mani et al, Unsupervised Selective Manifold Regularized Matrix Factorization, SDM 2021, and related work.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Local K-Means via KNN Graphs: An Efficient Approach for Large-Scale Clustering"

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a novel local k-means (LKM) algorithm leveraging KNN graphs to improve clustering efficiency. While the approach is interesting and supported by extensive empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets, significant methodological and presentation concerns prevent stronger endorsement. The core contributions are overshadowed by omissions in efficiency validation, unaddressed sensitivity issues, and technical inaccuracies that require revision.

### Strengths
*   **Comprehensive Empirical Evaluation:** The paper provides valuable experimental results across both synthetic and real datasets (Tables 1, 2, 3, Figures 2, 3), demonstrating LKM's performance relative to baselines under various conditions. This breadth strengthens the empirical foundation.

### Weaknesses and Major Concerns
Several critical weaknesses undermine the current presentation and claims:

1.  **Lack of Efficiency Validation:** A primary claimed advantage of LKM is computational efficiency. However, running times for the crucial real-world data experiments (Table 3) are entirely omitted. This absence severely weakens the central efficiency argument.
2.  **Unaddressed Initialization Sensitivity:** The paper fails to discuss or analyze the sensitivity of LKM to the initialization of the cluster assignments (y). Given the known sensitivity of standard k-means to initialization, this is a significant oversight for a method derived from it. The robustness of LKM remains unclear.
3.  **Insufficient Statistical Reporting:** For the synthetic data results (Table 1), it is unclear whether reported values are averages over multiple runs. Crucially, standard deviations are missing, making it impossible to assess the statistical significance or robustness of the observed performance differences.
4.  **Unclear Performance Interpretation:** The explanation for k-means' poor performance in Figure 3(b) is confusing. The figure visually suggests clusters are identified, yet the performance is deemed poor. This discrepancy requires a clear technical explanation.
5.  **Flawed Technical Definition:** The definition of "neighborhood" in Section 3 is technically incorrect and confusing. The relationship "x_j is a neighbor of x_i" is presented as symmetric (implying x_i is also a neighbor of x_j), which is not generally true for KNN graphs (e.g., directed or asymmetric k-NN). This fundamental definition needs correction.
6.  **Unhelpful Analogy:** The social network analogy used to introduce KNN graphs is not insightful and risks being misleading, as social networks often model symmetric relationships while KNN graphs frequently do not. It fails to add clarity or value.

### Suggestions for Improvement
To address the above concerns and strengthen the paper, the authors should:

1.  **Report and Discuss Running Times:** Include running times for all methods in Table 3 (real data experiments) and discuss these results in the context of LKM's efficiency claims.
2.  **Analyze Initialization Sensitivity:** Discuss the sensitivity of LKM to cluster initialization. Describe how initialization was handled in the experiments (e.g., multiple restarts, specific method) and present results demonstrating robustness (or lack thereof), potentially including variance due to initialization.
3.  **Enhance Synthetic Results Reporting:** Report results in Table 1 as averages over multiple runs (with the number of runs specified) and include standard deviations (or confidence intervals) to allow assessment of statistical significance and robustness.
4.  **Clarify Figure 3(b):** Provide a precise technical explanation for why k-means performs poorly in the scenario depicted in Figure 3(b), resolving the apparent contradiction with the visualization.
5.  **Correct Neighborhood Definition:** Revise the definition of "neighborhood" in Section 3 to accurately reflect the potentially asymmetric nature of neighbor relationships in KNN graphs. Explicitly state the graph construction method (e.g., mutual, symmetric, asymmetric) used.
6.  **Replace Social Network Analogy:** Remove the social network analogy. Instead, frame the KNN graph usage by relating it more directly to relevant prior work, such as exemplar-based clustering approaches or regularized NMF methods (e.g., cite and discuss connections to work like Mani et al., SDM 2021).

### Recommendation
**Decision: 6 - Marginally above the acceptance threshold**

**Justification:** The introduction of the LKM algorithm and the extensive empirical evaluation represent a contribution of potential interest to the community. However, the significant weaknesses outlined – particularly the lack of efficiency validation for real data, the unaddressed sensitivity to initialization, the flawed technical definition, and the insufficient statistical reporting – substantially diminish the paper's impact and clarity in its current form. Addressing the suggested revisions is essential to fully support the claims and solidify the contribution. While the paper is acceptable in principle, publication should be contingent on major revisions that comprehensively address these concerns.

extracted_ideas.summary: The review acknowledges the paper's introduction of a local k-means variant using KNN graphs and extensive empirical results, but highlights significant concerns about missing efficiency comparisons, initialization sensitivity, and technical clarity.
strengths: Extensive empirical results reported on both synthetic and real data
weaknesses: Running times for real data experiments (Table 3) are not reported, despite efficiency being a main advantage
LKM's dependence on initialization of clustering (y) is not discussed
Unclear if synthetic data results in Table 1 are averages over multiple runs; standard deviations not reported
Explanation of k-means' poor performance in Figure 3(b) is unclear (why clusters appear correctly identified but deemed poor?)
Definition of neighborhood in Section 3 is confusing and technically incorrect (neighbor relationship isn't symmetric)
Social network analogy for KNN graphs is deemed uninsightful and not equivalent
suggestions: Report and discuss running times for real data experiments (Table 3)
Discuss the sensitivity of LKM to initialization and how it was handled
Report results on synthetic data as averages with standard deviations over multiple runs
Clarify the explanation for k-means' poor performance in Figure 3(b)
Correct the definition/explanation of neighborhood relationships in Section 3
Replace the social network analogy with discussion of relevant work like exemplar-based approaches or regularized NMF methods (e.g., Mani et al., SDM 2021)
main_points: Critical lack of reported running times for real data undermines efficiency claims
Unaddressed sensitivity of LKM to initialization is a major oversight
Statistical reporting for synthetic results (averages, deviations) is insufficient
Technical definition of neighborhood in Section 3 is flawed (asymmetry issue)
Social network analogy is unhelpful and should be replaced with relevant literature
technical_details: Neighborhood definition flaw: x_j being a neighbor of x_i does not imply symmetry (x_i neighbor of x_j)
Need for standard deviations in Table 1 results to indicate robustness

reviews.summary: This submission introduces a computationally efficient clustering algorithm called LKM, which focuses on the scenarios where data is presented in the form of graph. In brief, only the k-nearest neighbour distances of each point are considered in the objective and a simple truncation of large distances is introduced to mitigate the effect of outliers.  The method is shown to produce promising practical performance in comparison with existing techniques both computationally and in terms of clustering performance.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

LKM is developed based on the k-means problem which is a fundamental problem in machine learning and the authors might bring some new insights and develop a new algorithm. The main strength of LKM lies in the practical aspects of the proposed algorithm, in that it is efficient and the performance is compelling. 
Specifically, the computational overhead is O(nk), where n and k denote the number of samples and nearest neighbors, respectively.

In addition, the unified framework of KSUMSC, and LCM also seems interesting. It revolves that SC, a popular clustering algorithm derived from graph cuts, can be optimized with the same time complexity as that of LKM.

Weaknesses:
1. Line 9: "Specifically....are denote the...". The sentence is grammatically incorrect, "are" should be removed.

2. Some works relating to the connection between k-means and clustering with graph cuts are not discussed in Section 2 (Related work).

3. The performance of SC optimized by the proposed algorithm is not shown.

4. What does \bar{y} stand for in Eq. (15) and (16)?

5. e_i should be a column vector.

6. Some steps in the derivations are not explained. What is the connection between (11) and (17)? 

7. The evaluation needs to be improved. The proposed clustering method uses ball-tree to construct a k-nearest neighbors graph as an intermediate step. Correspondingly, it is suggested to include the clustering performance of ball-tree as a baseline to compare.

8. Have the paper proof-read to remove grammatical errors

9. In page 7, line 178, "#Iter" should be "#Iter.".

10. In page 7, table 2, "FLK" should be "LKM".

11. References should be shown in the same format.

**comment after rebuttal**
I acknowledge that I read and other reviewers' comments and authors' responses. I would like to keep my score as most concerns of mine have been well addressed.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Linear k-Means Clustering for Efficient Graph Partitioning"

**Overall Assessment:**  
This paper presents a computationally efficient Linear k-Means (LKM) clustering algorithm for graph data, achieving O(nk) complexity. The work offers valuable insights, particularly the unified framework connecting LKM, Kernel k-means with unit norm and spherical constraint (KSUMS), and Spectral Clustering (SC), and demonstrates promising performance. While the core contributions are significant and merit acceptance, the manuscript requires substantial revisions to address technical clarity, presentation, and evaluation gaps. With these improvements, this will be a strong contribution. I recommend **acceptance after minor revisions** (Rating: 7 - Good paper, accept).

**Major Strengths:**  
1.  **Novel Algorithmic Contribution:** The paper effectively builds upon the fundamental k-means problem, introducing the LKM algorithm with new insights into efficient graph partitioning. The O(nk) computational overhead represents a significant practical advantage for large-scale graph data.
2.  **Compelling Performance:** The empirical evaluation shows that LKM achieves compelling clustering performance compared to several existing techniques, highlighting its practical utility.
3.  **Unified Framework:** The connection established between LKM, KSUMS, and SC is intellectually interesting and forms a key theoretical contribution. The revelation that SC can be optimized with the same O(nk) complexity as LKM is particularly noteworthy.

**Major Weaknesses and Required Revisions:**  
1.  **Technical Clarity and Derivations:**
    *   **Undefined Notation:** Critical notation remains undefined, especially `\bar{y}` in Equations (15) and (16). This must be explicitly defined.
    *   **Vector Specification:** The nature of `e_i` (e.g., column vector) is not specified and must be clarified.
    *   **Derivation Gaps:** The connection between the objective function derived in Equation (11) and the final form presented in Equation (17) lacks sufficient explanation. This logical progression needs elaboration to enhance reproducibility and understanding.
2.  **Incomplete Evaluation:**
    *   **Missing Baseline:** The evaluation uses ball-tree for k-NN graph construction but fails to benchmark against ball-tree k-means itself as a baseline. Including this comparison is essential for a fair assessment of LKM's efficiency and performance gains.
    *   **Lack of SC Results:** While the unified framework theoretically connects LKM and SC, and the paper states SC can be optimized with O(nk) complexity, no performance results for SC optimized using the proposed method are presented. Adding these results is crucial to validate this claim and demonstrate the framework's practical impact.
3.  **Presentation and Related Work:**
    *   **Related Work Gap:** The discussion of connections between k-means and graph cuts in the related work section is currently insufficient. This context is vital for positioning the novelty of LKM within the broader landscape; please expand this section.
    *   **Proofreading and Grammar:** Numerous grammatical errors (e.g., line 9) and typos detract from readability. A thorough proofread is mandatory. Specific instances include:
        *   Inconsistent reference formatting (standardize throughout).
        *   Formatting issues: '#Iter' should be '#Iter.' (page 7), 'FLK' should be 'LKM' (Table 2).
4.  **Minor Corrections:** Ensure all minor errors identified (grammar, formatting, table labels) are meticulously corrected.

**Recommendations for Revision:**  
1.  **Clarify Technical Details:** Explicitly define `\bar{y}` (Eq 15-16), specify `e_i` as a column vector, and provide a clearer, step-by-step explanation linking the derivation from Eq 11 to the objective in Eq 17.
2.  **Strengthen Evaluation:** Add benchmark results comparing LKM against ball-tree k-means. Include performance results for Spectral Clustering optimized using the proposed O(nk) method.
3.  **Enhance Related Work:** Expand the discussion on the connections between k-means and graph cut problems to better frame the contribution.
4.  **Improve Presentation:** Conduct a thorough proofread to eliminate all grammatical errors and typos. Correct the formatting issues ('#Iter.' , 'LKM' in Table 2). Standardize reference formatting throughout the manuscript.

**Conclusion:**  
The LKM algorithm presents a valuable and efficient approach to graph clustering with strong empirical performance and a theoretically significant unified framework. The O(nk) complexity is a major practical strength. However, the manuscript's impact is currently hampered by issues in technical exposition, evaluation completeness, and presentation quality. Addressing the outlined weaknesses through careful revision is essential. Once these revisions are made, this paper will be a solid contribution to the field. I recommend **acceptance after minor revisions**.

reviews.summary: The authors use a reformulation of the k-means objective to propose an algorithm that minimizes that objective (or some relaxation thereof), and then show some experiments that indicate promising clustering performance.  Currently I believe the paper needs revision before acceptance: the empirical results are not particularly compelling as they omit runtimes on real-world datasets, and the theoretical results do not factor in or bound the number of iterations of the algorithm.
reviews.main_review:
Hello authors,

Thank you for your submission.  I enjoyed reading it and I think there are some nice ideas contained in the paper.  However, I have a number of concerns that keep me from recommending acceptance for this paper.  Primarily, my biggest concern is the given runtime bound (which I find inaccurate as it does not count the number of iterations), and the experimental evaluation: runtime analysis is a very important measure of the utility of an algorithm, and this is missing for the real-world datasets.

More details below (in no particular order):

1. The computational complexity does not consider the number of iterations before convergence.  Therefore I find the claim that the algorithm takes $O(nc)$ time (or rather $O(n(k + c))$ where $c$ is the number of clusters and $k$ is the number of distances known per point) misleading or incorrect.  In fact, Lloyd's algorithm also takes $O(nc)$ per iteration, but the number of iterations is not easily bounded.  Unless the authors can conclusively show that the number of iterations should not be considered in the runtime, this claim must be modified or retracted.

2. As pointed out in the introduction, the initialization of k-means can make a huge difference for performance.  However, on line 204 the authors simply write "K-means is initialized in a random way"---but what is this random way?  If I were to, e.g., randomly assign points to clusters to initialize, this can lead to very poor results and slow convergence, at least for Lloyd's algorithm.  Even
running many trials of this strategy will produce poor performance for every strategy.  Thus proper initialization is very important.  It would be very helpful if the authors could provide more information on this point.

3. The authors have not provided any information on runtime on real-world datasets.  Thus it is difficult to evaluate the utility of the proposed algorithm, LKM.  Clustering algorithms often behave very differently on diverse datasets, and thus I think that runtimes only on the toy dataset---and only comparing with Lloyd's algorithm---does not give a full picture.  In fact, the number of iterations on the toy datasets is very small when compared to what Lloyd's algorithm will do on higher-dimensional real datasets (where cluster boundaries are not so clear-cut).  For the sake of clarity, the authors should address the issue of runtime comprehensively on real-world datasets.

4. It would also be nice to include some information on the dimensionality and number of points in each dataset listed in 4.2.1 in the main paper, if possible.  I appreciate that 16 datasets are used---this seems like a fairly comprehensive set of experiments---but I suspect most readers will not have an idea of what the characteristics of these datasets are, and many will not look at the
supplemental material.  When I consult the supplementary material, I see that many of the datasets used are relatively high-dimensional; the minimum dimension appears to be 256.  I would suggest that the authors also try on lower-dimensional data (e.g. below 50 dimensions), as convergence characteristics of different algorithms tend to be very different in lower dimensions.

5. A note about the limitations: there are many techniques to quickly construct k-NN graphs.  In lower dimensions, this can be done via trees (see, e.g., [1-3]); in higher dimensions, this can be done approximately via hashing (e.g., [4-5]).  In fact even if only a similarity function is used, so long as that function is a kernel, additional branch-and-bound techniques could be used ([6-8]).  Anyway, perhaps you will find these interesting, if you were not already aware of those lines of work.

6. When comparing Lloyd's algorithm to LKM, note also that there are several variants of Lloyd's algorithm that produce exactly the same result in accelerated time.  The authors even cite some (reference [11]).  There are more however, especially Elkan's [9] and Hamerly's [10] that perform very well in practice that should probably be considered in an empirical comparison.  Personally, if I am doing k-means in practice, I often don't bother with the standard Lloyd algorithm because Elkan's or Hamerly's variants (or the
tree-based ones) are so much faster---it can often be an orders-of-magnitude speedup.

7. Can more detail be provided in Algorithm 1 on how y is initialized randomly?  Is the algorithm uniformly randomly drawing a cluster assignment for each point?

8. I think the paper could benefit from some careful proofreading.  There are numerous grammatical issues, especially having to do with tenses (present/past/etc.).

9. The suggestion in Eq. (10) to convert a (non-metric) similarity function to a dissimilarity function still produces a non-metric: it is not guaranteed to satisfy the triangle inequality.  This makes the change from Eq. (8) (which is built on distances using D) to Eq. (11) (which is built on dissimilarities using G) significant: particularly, I am no longer certain that we are solving the same problem as (3).  I am okay with the fact that LKM solves a relaxation of (3) but I think this should be explicitly pointed out.  On the other hand, note that if the similarity function is a kernel, then it *does* induce a distance metric in kernel space, and that could be used instead.  Specifically, Eq. (2.2) in [8] uses this induced distance metric.

[1] "N-Body'problems in statistical learning", NIPS 2001
[2] "Tree-independent dual-tree algorithms", ICML 2013
[3] "Random projection trees and low dimensional manifolds", STOC 2008
[4] "Near-optimal hashing algorithms for approximate nearest neighbor in high
dimensions", FOCS '06
[5] "LSH Forest: Practical Algorithms Made Theoretical", SODA 2017
[6] "Asymmetric LSH (ALSH) for sublinear time maximum inner product search (MIPS)", NIPS 2014
[7] "Maximum inner-product search using cone trees", KDD 2012
[8] "Fast Exact Max-Kernel Search", SDM 2013
[9] "Using the triangle inequality to accelerate k-means", ICML 2003
[10] "Making k-means even faster", SDM 2010

I hope that these comments are helpful!
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I don't think that a new clustering algorithm has either positive or negative societal impact.  So everything seems fine here.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
